scenario: I can't move my left arm

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> OK. Uh, and how's your arm right now?"] Um. Well like, I feel no pain but just, uh, can't move it.
[Clinician asked: "That you still can't move. And, and what time did you wake up? You said it was an hour, you're sure about that?"] Yes.
[Clinician asked: "OK, um. Now, can I just double check? Has this ever happened before?"] No. No, uh, it's really the first time.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And, did you sleep, anything,  in an awkward position, or in a different bed, or anything like that last night?"] Uh no, uhf, everything as usual.

[Clinician asked: "K, um, does your hand feel cold?"] Mmm, no, really the, mmm, the arm feels fine, apart from me not being able to move it.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And it's to your  on your left side. Have you had any symptoms in your left leg at all?"] No, but um, I feel a bit of tingling on the left side of my face.

[Clinician asked: "Right, OK. Um, and how long has that tingling been there for?"] Uh, since I woke up. Um, since I started to feel the <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> , since, since the the arm really it stopped moving, so an hour ago.

[Clinician asked: "OK, um. Now, um, I can't see you.  It's, it's telephone appointment. Have you noticed any kind of, um, asymmetry in your face, or did your face expression look different on one side compared to the other, or anything different at all?"] Um. Mmm, no, not really, it's just. Uh, having like  a bit of issues like, uh, I was trying to drink earlier and I'd be bold a bit and and the tingling on, on the left side.
[Clinician asked: "OK. So, um, you have some difficulties swallowing, is that right?"] Um, yeah.

[Clinician asked: "OK. Any problems with your speech? Is your, is your, is your speech different to how it, um, sounds normally?"] Um, yeah it feels a, a bit harder . It's more complicated maybr, maybe um, be more slow.  I need to think more about it.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Is there anyone at home with you?"] Uh, no.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] Is this something I should be worried about?
[Clinician asked: "Um, well, um, I'm  I'm concerned that some of the symptoms you've got may be, um, symptoms of a stroke."] Hmm, wow.

[Clinician asked: "OK? So, um, that's something that can be dealt with, but it needs to be dealt with as an emergency in a hospital."] Would, should I call , would you be able to call the ambulance for me?

[Clinician asked: "Yeah I'm, I'm happy to do that. Um, what we need to do is to make sure that they've got access to you when you come. So, um, maybe you could, uh, leave the front door, um, unlocked, sit in the hallway. But I'm gonna arrange one of my colleagues next to me to, um, call an ambulance to your home addre"] OK. OK.  OK.  Thank you.
[Clinician asked: "So  that's being done right now, as we speak. Uh, while we're waiting for that, I'm just gonna ask you a few more questions. Is that OK?"] Sure.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Do you have any other illnesses at all?"] Um. I have, uh, type two diabetes and, uh, high blood pressure.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And, um, are you on any medication for that?"] Um, I'm taking  Metformin losartan and Amlodipine.

[Clinician asked: "OK, anyone else?"] Not really.
[Clinician asked: "And um,  are you normally living alone?"] Uh, yes.

[Clinician asked: "OK. OK. All right, um. How are you feeling now? Have you got the symptoms right now still, that, um"] Yes, yes. The the arm still can't move and I think the speech is getting a bit harder.

gold_note:
Hx:  Difficulty moving left arm, occurred upon waking this morning
Feels no pain, but still can't move it
No previous similar episodes
Slept as per normal last night
No discolouration in hand, arm doesn't feel cold
Symptoms limited to left arm, not in left leg
Tingling present on left side of face over the same period (1 hour)
Patient has not noticed any facial asymmetry
Difficulty swallowing water- dribbled water
Speech seems ‘harder' or ‘slow'
No-one at home with patient
Pmhx: T2dm, HTN
DH: metformin, losartan, amlodipine
Allergic to penicillin
FH: father hs ‘heart disease'
SH: lives alone, ex smoker, mild- moderate alcohol intake.
Imp: ? CVA
LAS called- blue light, pt to keep front door open, will stay on line till LAS arrives and hand over.

naive.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns with me, Jack. I understand how alarming this must feel, especially when you're experiencing symptoms like difficulty moving your arm, tingling on your face, and challenges with swallowing and speech. These symptoms can be signs of a serious condition, such as a stroke, which requires immediate medical attention.

I've already arranged for an ambulance to be on its way, and they will take you to the hospital where you'll receive the care you need. Please make sure the front door is unlocked and stay near it so the paramedics can access you easily. The medical team there is prepared to assess you and provide the necessary treatment.

You're doing the right thing by seeking help quickly, and I'm here to support you. The ambulance should arrive shortly, and they will ensure you get the care you need. Take a deep breath, stay calm, and know that help is on the way. You're not alone in this.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns with me. It’s completely understandable that you’re worried, especially with symptoms like sudden arm weakness, facial tingling, and speech difficulty. These could be signs of a stroke or transient ischemic attack (TIA), which are serious conditions that require immediate attention.

Given your symptoms and medical history, including type 2 diabetes and high blood pressure, it’s crucial to seek emergency care. Conditions like diabetes and hypertension can increase stroke risk, so it’s important to address this promptly. I recommend calling an ambulance right away to get to the nearest emergency room. The medical team there can perform the necessary evaluations, such as imaging, to determine the cause and appropriate treatment.

While waiting for the ambulance, try to stay calm and remain in a safe place. Once at the hospital, they’ll assess you thoroughly. After your evaluation, we can discuss further steps. Please let me know if you have any questions or need assistance before the ambulance arrives. Your health and safety are our top priorities.

framework_only.response_text:

We'll arrange for an ambulance to come to your home as we speak. Please prepare to go to the hospital for further assessment by the stroke team.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Once you're at the hospital, the doctors will assess your condition and determine the next steps. We'll follow up with you as soon as we have more information.
